anchor.snippet: type theory is the study of formal systems that classify expressions or mathematical objects by their types
Russell's paradox which demonstrates that, without proper axioms, it is possible to define the set of all sets that are not members of themselves

annotations.label: Type theory as a mathematical logic

anchor.snippet: is a collection of rules of inference that result in judgments
A term in logic is recursively defined as a constant symbol , variable , or a function application
Most type theories have 4 judgments:
Judgments may follow from assumptions.
The list of assumptions on the left is the context of the judgment.
A type theory's inference rules say what judgments can be made, based on the existence of other judgments.
For example, the following inference rule states a substitution rule for judgmental equality.
The applied rules form a proof tree , where the top-most rules need no assumptions.
One example of a rule that does not require any inputs is one that states the type of a constant term.
Generally, the desired conclusion of a proof in type theory is one of type inhabitation
Girard's paradox shows that type inhabitation is strongly related to the consistency of a type system with Curry–Howard correspondence.
Also, for each "by rule" type, there are 4 different kinds of rules:
Because the law of excluded middle does not hold, there is no term of type
Constructive mathematics requires when proving
An example of a non-constructive proof is proof by contradiction.
The Curry–Howard correspondence is the observed similarity between logics and programming languages.

annotations.label: Locally cartesian closed categories correspond to Martin-Löf type theories

anchor.snippet: locally cartesian closed categories correspond to Martin-Löf type theories (Seely, 1984).
cubical type theory was proposed, which is a homotopy type theory with normalization
The most basic types are called atoms, and a term whose type is an atom is known as an atomic term.

The unit type has exactly 1 canonical term.
The Boolean type has exactly 2 canonical terms.

annotations.label: Set theory has rules and axioms; type theory only rules

anchor.snippet: Set theory has both rules and axioms , while type theories only have rules.

annotations.label: Classical logic has excluded middle; type theory need not

anchor.snippet: Classical set theory and logic have the law of excluded middle

annotations.label: Set membership vs. single-typing of terms

anchor.snippet: In set theory, an element is not restricted to one set.
Type theory has a built-in notion of computation.

annotations.label: Set theory encodes numbers as sets

anchor.snippet: Set theory encodes numbers as sets

annotations.label: Proofs have types in type theory

anchor.snippet: In type theory, proofs have types whereas in set theory, proofs are part of the underlying first-order logic.
Many types of theories are strongly normalizing , which means that any order of applying the rules will always end in the same result.
Some combinations of types are equivalent to other combinations of types.
Most type theories do not have axioms
"Axiom K" ensures "uniqueness of identity proofs".
"Univalence axiom" holds that equivalence of types is equality of types.

annotations.label: Axiom of choice derivable in type theory

anchor.snippet: The axiom of choice does not need to be added to type theory, because in most type theories it can be derived from the rules of inference.
